sentences.text: Three sisters.
Yeah.
Well in the beginning from the war, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's very panic, a very big panic.
You know, when they came in, they started right away.
I have to tell you a short story, which is very interesting.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was--when I was in jail, I know my friend, the same age from me, he was a communist.
And he, every Saturday, come to us just for eat fish, Jewish fish.
He so love it.
Fresh halibut.
My mother bake it, and Jewish fish.
He always loved it.
We were the best friends.
When the war break out, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he-- I didn't know where he is, because we look out for ourselves, everybody for ourselves.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A few days later, I find out he, in the communist-- he is in the Hitler Youth.
He was a German.
And we didn't know.
He wore the red band with the Hakenkreuz, and looking for communist.
He looked for me, too.
And he came to us in-- I was not home.
And I was by an uncle-- they call this in Lodz the ballot.
This is the-- the-- a section where was the ghetto was after that.
And I had an uncle there, and I was by him, hiding out.
I came home, and I decided to run away because after when they took us to the police station to clean, and they beat me up and I got-- damaged my ear, then I decided to run away to Russia.
Now the thing is, what I told you, one guy in the jail, but I give him the whole food when I'm sitting there.
And he is a delegate to the [INAUDIBLE] industry to the Communist Party.

When I run to there to the-- the-- a rumor was there, don't go to the station, but by the train. who's going to the station, all Jews are arrested, and they're put in cattle trains, and they send it away somewhere.
We didn't know at that time Auschwitz or something like this one.
So the best thing is to go by bus.
And the bus there is far, a little out from the city.
So I took my package and everything what my mother gave to me, and I started.
With a small satchel, I went to the bus to buy a ticket to go to Russia-- from Russia, to go to my uncle.
My mother got a brother there.
He was an engineer on carburetors.
And from there, to go to the other side to Russia.
On the way, people started to run back.
When I talked with them why, you not can get a ticket.
Jews, they're not selling ticket to the bus.
And I give a look, the friend, what I give the whole food with my mother and father-- send it to me, stayed by our building, and hold a baby under-- under his arm.
And I show him-- there-- Adam's his name.
I said Adam, do me a favor and purchase-- buy me a ticket for the bus in [NON-ENGLISH], And he said, you dirty Jew.
And the-- the other baby, took it in the other hand, and then I realized he wears a red arm band with the Hakenkreuz.
Good, he got the baby in his hand, and I was younger than him, so I run.
I lost the small suitcase.
And I run away, and then I realize what this means-- the Polack.
He was a communist, he was with me in jail.
I gave him all my food.
And when it's coming to beat up a Jew, he was number one.
The Germans have to pay a lot of the Polacks to pay the price for this one.
Now what I wanted to say, you not can rely, nor on yourself.
And this was a time what I run away from home.
I ran away to Russia.
I went the next time we went back to the train-- to the bus, it's impossible.
Now, the train is too many people to hold control of them.
So I smuggled over with my cousin.
He looked more German like Jew.
I looked like a Jew.
Now, my cousin has looked more German, or a Polack, like a Jew.
He buy me a ticket, for him and for myself.
We went in in the train.
And on the train, I came to Russia.
We went to my uncle, but my uncle and his cousin-- my uncle on my mother's side.
And the uncle is my mother's brother.
He was an engineer, like a chemical engineer.
He's lent himself, and the Germans need him for making carburetors to cars.
For this reason, he was out the ghetto, when the ghetto was there for a time.
When I went to him overnight and I said, I won't go over to Russia, I want to stay a little, he said no, run away.
A German officer came to him and told him nobody to know that all Jews will be arrested and sent away in concentration camp.
They didn't said they're going to kill us like Auschwitz.
In this time, they haven't got Auschwitz.
So the next-- I came in the evening.
The next day in the morning, about 7 o'clock, when it's legal to go out the street, I went to another train, went to the border with Russia.
And where did that-- where did you get off that train?
I forgot the name.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I forgot the name.
We went off right on the border.
And the Germans started to take off a lot of people, but the Germans got a deal with the Russians at that time.
At that time, they was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So when I run out from the train, I got no-- no suitcase, no nothing.
We run in the woods.
And from the woods, run to the border.
And over the border, we went over to Bialystock.
Bialystock is the other side from the border.
I lost my cousin, what I said, what I went with him.
His name is Alex Silwer.
He's now in Israel.
I lost him under the way, and after I find out he is alive, you know, because we never see each other after this.
He went one way, I went the other way.
And I was in a city on the border side called Volodymyr-Volynskyi, in the beginning.
Or I-- from Bialystock, I went there, and I look for work.
I have to make a living.
And I left in the home my three sisters, with my mother-- I left home.
And I started to think how to bring them here.
At least, they got bread.
Bread they got, and they sleep all together in the shul, in the synagogue.
You know, or-- I need a family, you know.
And I was single.
One time, after a short time, they started to-- the Russians started to evacuate the border towns from the refugees.
And they sent to Russia, deep Russia.
They started a rumor that they send us for slave labor like, say.
Being there, I realized that the communism is not this-- what their propaganda is.
Feeding the people and the soldiers, how they're dressed, and how they eat, and how they look.
We have realized that it's not so like they say.
When they evacuated to our city, they call Zdolbunov-- Zdolbunov is a border town between the old Russia and Ukraine, what had belonged to Poland.
Well, Ukraine was divided between Russia and Poland.
Zdolbunov-- Zdolbunov got-- I not remember the tracks for the trains-- that is one side wider, and one side narrow.
So a train for Zdolbunov never can go to Russia, and a Russian's train never can go to Zdolbunov to Poland.
So they've got like-- how they call it-- like that one and this side one track, and this side other track.
Yes.
The ramp, how they call this?
It's like a table.
You know, you go across from train to the other side train?
Platform.
Platform.
You know, it's out from the-- so I supposed the people that came there with the train, would evacuate for deep to Russia, they have to go over the platform to the other train.
I was very thin and small.
So between the trains, I went down on the floor under the train, under the platform, and I run over to the wood, to the bushes there.
After when the people went, the train went to Russia with the people, and the other train going back to Poland, to the center of Ukraine.
I was in the bush there overnight.
And the next day, I was shivering for cold.
In the middle of the day, like in the evening, I give a look, my sister-- what today, is she's not alive-- they met her husband.
When they realized what is going on, my sister was married to .
Rappoport They married, and my mother said go away, run away.
So they run to Ukraine, and I didn't know.
They looking for me, and I didn't know to look for them.
But I didn't-- they know I run to Russia.
But I didn't know they run away from Poland.
When I saw my sister, I said, Chaya, what you doing?
My sister's name is Chaya.
She fainted seeing me.
And I was so-- in dirty clothes, not shaved, not hair.
If I would not said, Chaya, to call her name, she would-- she would not recognize me.
My brother-in-law was a little in business there-- told me not to go out from the wood.
He bring right away in-- you know, there's no cars there.
A horse with a carriage there, he brings.
And they cover their carriage, and they put me inside.
They bring with them some-- I took a bath, I showered and I shaved, in a suit-- the next day, I got a suit what fit me.
In the same day, I got a passport to-- I was-- to be legal in the city.
My brother-in-law got connections there-- and for money, you can do everything.
So I got a passport and I was legal the next day.
So like that, nothing.
Was the passport in your name?
Yeah.
And my picture, even.
So it was legal with that passport--
I was a legal-- you know, in everything, forget it, what just happened yesterday.
Where is mother and where are the other sisters?
She didn't know, and I didn't know.
I just know my sister here, with my brother-in-law.
My brother-in-law was-- you know, the train got special stores from the government to supply the people what got-- belonged to the party, to the Communist Party.
And working on the-- at the train station.
You know, like mechanics, just everything, you know?
And he was the manager from the stores.
There's no ownership, but there is manager.
And the stores in the-- in the-- what they have got there in the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you can get everything.
You name it, and they've got it there.
Everything for them.
For the people, nothing-- not for them.
So my brother-in-law, not to make anti-Semitism like there, so he went to a factory what make it ice cream, For work, he got ice cream.
And I got, you know, a push cart, by hand, with the three, so, inside cans with ice cream.
And I went to the station where the trains came.
I yell out, one ruble an ice cream.
One ruble an ice cream.
All of us are lining up.
I suppose for the three cans of ice cream, I supposed to supply 300 rubles for every can ice cream on the [NON-ENGLISH].
Which means 100 ice cream in a can.
And I got in every can, I got 250 ice cream.
And I remember like now being there, their secretary look for the payment.
You're supposed to give us 300 ruble.
And I got them even 900 ruble.
And I start to give most of that.
No, no, no, no, no, no.
This is for you. 300 ruble.
And I got left over maybe 500 ruble.
So I started to make money there, and every day I went to the train with ice cream, in the morning to the evening, once and twice.
And I sold it and I called, one ruble an ice cream, one ruble!
And I got three assortments of ice cream-- like vanilla and red, they make it with syrup, and some other kind with some walnuts.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
And after, when the war came, started the war-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my-- the-- I remember my sister and my brother told me, take it how much you have.
If what you have, take it and run away.
I say, why you not?
They got so much money, we're going to come, our mother, and his parents, they have so much money-- they have gold and diamonds in there.
To bring it home, they will save the mother in a-- and they didn't realize.
And the moment when we run away, the last train, I went deep to deep Russia.
The last train.
And they wait there in the moment when the last train went, the Ukrainian people, rounded up all Jews there, and they killed them before the Germans came in.
Before the-- ha, I notice everything.
After the war, when I went home, I have to go through the same city, Zdolbunov.
Because Zdolbunov is the key train station, what exchange the trains.
So when I came from Russia, and I wanted to go to Poland, I have to go through the station Zdolbunov, the same thing, to change from the platform to another train.
Knowing the city, and working on the station, you know, when I sold the ice cream, I know every door and everything.
Never mind it's bombed-- or-- I know everything.
I got time, enough time, to go around and find out what happened to the Rappoports?
What did happen to [NON-ENGLISH]?
What did happen to this and this one?
And I find out what's happened.
The last train, as, when we left, and before the Germans came in, they took out all Jews and they killed.
I call them, but my sister and my brother-in-law, they not remember.
And they just said, here and here that all Jews is there.
In the short [NON-ENGLISH], is a big, big mass grave-- and there have to be my sister, my brother-in-law.
The Ukrainians did it before even the Germans came in.
They did the same crime, but before there were other communist.
I beg your pardon.
Yeah.
Deep Russia, the trains would travel just, you know, I-- to be clear, I tell you.
I run deep to Russia, boarded a lot to-- to stop before.
The old trains, they gone-- go, go, go, go, go like this one.
We're gone for weeks-- took us to go to the Middle East, to Middle-- Samarkand.
Coming in Samarkand-- I got no suitcase, no nothing.
Nothing was left over.
I'm just like a beggar come from the sky.
Though I try [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to do something.
I decided to go to school.
The build-- in the school, they've got the dormitory.
You know a dormitory.
But they paid 120 rubles every student for the stipend, they call this.
For the 120 rubles, you can buy the half a bread in that time.
So everything-- the price is.
Or this is the salary normal time what they paid every student.
He got 120 rubles for 405 and then they have to steal.
Anyway, I went to school, to the called the institute-- the Institute for Agriculture Institute.
I was learning there and I feel very good, you know.
I got a shirt-- I sold it.
I got a tie-- I sold it.
Used to leave-- hungered a little, or we got a roof over our head.
We sleep like in shelves there.
I remember like now eight people in a small room, with the cupboard, the clothing cupboards and maybe a small-- Me look for friend.
Me got Ukrainian friend, Jewish friend.
There were only two-- we feel sorry for the war was now coming, and we wanted to fit into the people.
We've got no choice.
We wanted to live.
Probably yes.
I said this to somebody, I have a brother in America.
After the war I will go there, and they're so good in America.
Maybe I said this.
Probably I said this one.
And Ukraine girl, I still remember Mashosja her name, she wanted to marry me.
And I rejected, because my mind was to go to America.
She is Ukrainian.
And I rejected her-- I rejected even to go out with her.
One night in the time, sleeping in the dormitory, the dormitory, I'm sorry, the pronouncing is--
Then come some, in civil clothing, and they wake me up.
You Peretz Rotenburg?
I said, yes.
Come on down.
I said, what?
Shh, he said, don't say nothing.
Shh-- it's the middle of the night.
I say, what?
Come on down.

And when I went down, and looking down, I saw he with a gun revolver-- and the Russian, the undercover there with gun, with revolver was so bent there, like from leather made or some-- not a [INAUDIBLE], leather, but holding the gun there.
So I said OK, I will take mine few things that I got in a bag, like a shirt, some of that.
He said, you not need it.
You not need it.
You come right away back.
We wanted you to find out something.
They took me in the station, I never went back.
I lost everything.
And they said, you make propaganda-- America propaganda.
I am a spy.
I said, what kind of spy I am?
You have run away a witness, the girl.
She went to the undercover there, and she said that I said here is no good, and communism is no good.
He will go to America, in he will say the true.
And I said, I didn't say nothing.
I just said I have a brother there, and after the war I will go there.
I am not a Russian.
Never mind, I get me 10 years.
[NON-ENGLISH]-- This means 10 years, or death penalty, because I was an American spy.
I was sent in different lagers.
And I wanted to say only thing.
I not call this concentration camp.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Me not can call this concentration camp in the German concentration camp.
This is working places-- it's true, but not concentration camp.
But in the lager where I was, I was a tailor.
I can say I got more food and more better sleep from that-- not freedom.
Now food, and a roof over my head.
Better like I would got other side.
I got lots of food.
Even money I make in the lager.
But a lot of the officers, for they came in-- I remember one officer, [NON-ENGLISH], he's a Russian.
He came in, he wanted to make him in the pants special pockets.
I make him special pockets.
He bring me in a whole bag of rice.
This is like a billion dollars.
And I said, I wanted tobacco to smoke.
He bring me in real cigarettes, which you not see this in Russia.
In Russia, you've got just [NON-ENGLISH] with paper to roll.
And he bring me in a real cigarette.
Well he got this-- you know, the officers, the soldiers-- they've got lots of good things, because they're in the army.
They're in the fronts.
They're entitled to this one.
So he sending some other guys, too.
And I make them secret pockets they needed, you know.
And I did everything, and they paid me.
They give me a lot of food.
What year was it that you were arrested?
I was arrested and sent to lager-- 10 years.
Oh.
This was in 19-- this is interesting.
This is in 1942.
Yeah.
In 1942 I was arrested.
I got 10 years.
Yeah.
I was-- in the school, I didn't spend much time as I was arrested.
No.
I wanted to say another one thing.
I wish my parents, my sisters, be in Russia.
Never mind the anti-Semitism.
This everything.
You see it in Germany by the Germans it's enough to be a Jew.
You have no other choice.
You've got no other way to be killed, because you are Jewish.
In Germany, you haven't got that life.
And if some from the Jewish people saying the Nazis are not agreeing with them because the Germans did this with the Nazis. 95% of the Germans, they support Nazis.
They support Hitler.
The Germans, they spent millions of dollars just to wash our mind, wash our brains to say the Nazis.
And after 50 years or 100 years, or our great-grandchildren, will say Nazis, they will know we're Nazis-- Polish Nazis, or American Nazis, or some other Nazis.
They will never know how did the Germans-- the Nazis.
The Germans did this with the Nazis.
With the Polish Nazis, with the Romanian Nazis.
Or the German, they're the leader.
Germany is responsible.
Oh yeah, oh yeah, oh yeah.
Yeah.
Some other-- some from other kinds crime for stealing, for dealing, which is not legal there to buy and sell.
They did that.
The market is always, oh, boy, the black market was full of it.
No, they'd have to have arrested people.
They needed working people.
So they arrested like-- they went out and they found 100 people to arrest.
There is 100 people.
You guilty or not guilty is not the point.
They get you [NON-ENGLISH]-- [NON-ENGLISH], this means a number why you're guilty.
Yeah.
Yeah.
